title: The BPTT Process Recap
concept: BPTT updates the same shared weights across all unrolled time steps based on sequential loss. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Vanishing Gradients in RNNs.'

exercises.prompt: What type of data are Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) specifically designed to process?

options.text: Sequential data like text or time-series

exercises.explain: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are designed to process sequential data, like text, audio, or time-series data, step-by-step.

cards.title: Recapping BPTT
cards.body: We know that Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) reuse the same weights at every step. But how do they actually learn from their mistakes across time? They use a clever technique called Backpropagation Through Time, or BPTT! Let's review how it works.

cards.title: What is BPTT?
cards.body: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the algorithm used to train recurrent neural networks. It works by "unrolling" the network across time steps, calculating the error (loss) at each step, and summing up the gradients to update the single set of shared weights that the network uses at every step.

cards.title: The Movie Actor Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a single actor playing the same character in five different scenes of a movie. At the end of the film, the director gives feedback. Instead of changing the actor for each scene, the director gives the actor notes on how to adjust their acting style, which changes how they play the character across the entire movie. In BPTT, the actor is the set of shared weights, and the scenes are the time steps!

cards.title: Tracking a Sentence
cards.body: Consider a 3-word sentence: 'Cats love milk.' The RNN processes 'Cats', then 'love', then 'milk'. If it makes a mistake predicting the final word, BPTT traces the error backward from 'milk' to 'love' and then to 'Cats'. It uses the total error to update the shared weights so the network predicts better next time.

cards.title: The Shared Weights Myth
cards.body: People often think that because an RNN is unrolled into many steps, each step gets its own unique weights. This is a myth! There is only one set of shared weights. BPTT updates this single set of weights using the combined errors from all steps.

cards.title: Standard Backprop vs. BPTT
cards.body: Let's visualize the difference between normal Backpropagation and Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).

left.text: Updates independent weights in a feedforward network where data only flows straight through.

right.text: Updates shared weights in a recurrent network by tracing errors backward through unrolled time steps.

exercises.prompt: What does the acronym BPTT stand for?
exercises.explain: BPTT stands for Backpropagation Through Time. It is the core algorithm used to train Recurrent Neural Networks on sequential data.

exercises.prompt: True or False: During BPTT, the gradients from all time steps are accumulated to update a single set of shared weights.
exercises.explain: True. Even if an RNN is unrolled over 100 steps, there is only one physical set of weights that gets updated using the total gradient.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the statement about RNN training.
exercises.sentence: BPTT updates the same ___ weights across all unrolled time steps.
exercises.explain: Because an RNN uses the exact same weights at every step, we call them shared weights. BPTT updates these shared weights using the combined error.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following descriptions accurately explains the BPTT process?

options.text: BPTT is a forward-pass algorithm that ignores past states and only calculates predictions for the current instant without weights.
BPTT unrolls the recurrent network over time, calculates loss at the steps, and sums the gradients to update the shared weights.
BPTT creates a quantum temporal feedback loop that duplicates weights into parallel dimensions to prevent loss calculations.

exercises.explain: Option A describes standard backpropagation in feedforward networks. Option B is correct because BPTT unrolls the loop over time and accumulates gradients to update shared weights. Option C is AI-generated gibberish about quantum temporal loops.

exercises.prompt: When training an RNN, how does Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) handle the shared weights across different time steps?

options.text: It replaces the weights at each time step with a completely brand-new set of random numbers.
It accumulates the gradients across all unrolled time steps to update the single set of shared weights.
It updates only the weights of the very first step and leaves the rest of the steps unchanged.
It duplicates the weights into separate, unique matrices that no longer share any values.

exercises.explain: Because an RNN uses the exact same shared weight matrix at every single time step, Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) must calculate the error gradients across all steps and combine them to update that single, shared set of weights. Next up: Vanishing Gradients in RNNs.
